132	### Phase 4: Evaluation Corpus
133	
134	Use the combined tool insight to tune Vale rules against real DDx docs.
135	
136	Work:
137	
138	- Create a labeled corpus under `cli/internal/docprose/testdata/corpus/`.
139	- Include real DDx excerpts and synthetic edge cases.
140	- Label each expected issue:
141	  - useful finding
142	  - false positive
143	  - intentionally dense technical prose
144	  - token-cost reduction candidate
145	  - missed AI-slop construction
146	- Include negative samples for paths, commands, tables, IDs, frontmatter, API
147	  names, acceptance criteria, and measured claims.
148	- Record expected findings in golden JSON.
149	
150	Acceptance:
151	
152	- Corpus tests compare normalized DDx findings, not Vale raw output.
153	- Good technical samples stay quiet.
154	- AI-slop samples produce line-specific findings.
155	- Token-cost samples identify removable filler without changing meaning.
156	- Full `docs/**` scan reports a plausible number of findings and no broad
157	  technical-structure noise.
158	
159	Corpus contents:
160	
161	| Corpus group | Purpose | Minimum cases |
162	|---|---|---:|
163	| `positive/ai-slop` | Common LLM constructions that sound polished but omit useful detail. | 12 |
164	| `positive/unsupported-claim` | Broad claims that need actor/action/evidence. | 10 |
165	| `positive/token-cost` | Sentences where shorter wording is strictly better. | 10 |
166	| `positive/missing-actor-action` | Abstract "enables/supports/streamlines" sentences. | 8 |
167	| `negative/technical-density` | Dense but useful DDx technical prose. | 10 |
168	| `negative/markdown-structure` | Tables, commands, paths, links, frontmatter, IDs, code spans. | 12 |
169	| `negative/evidence-backed-claim` | Strong claims backed by tests, benchmarks, measurements, or explicit constraints. | 8 |
170	| `real/ddx-docs` | Excerpts from existing DDx docs with reviewed labels. | 30 |
171	
172	Evaluation metrics:
173	
174	| Metric | Gate |
175	|---|---|
176	| Positive recall | At least 80% of labeled positive cases produce the expected DDx rule id. |
177	| Negative quiet rate | At least 90% of negative cases produce no finding. |
178	| Structure false positives | 0 findings in code spans, fenced blocks, paths, commands, frontmatter, and DDx IDs unless the case is explicitly positive. |
179	| Token-cost precision | At least 80% of token-cost findings are judged shorter-without-meaning-loss. |
180	| Real-doc usefulness | A manual review of the full-doc scan marks at least 70% of findings useful. |
181	| Finding volume | Full `docs/**` scan is sparse enough for review: target under 1 finding per 1,000 words until rules prove higher precision. |
182	
183	How we know it works:
184	
185	- Golden tests lock expected normalized findings for the corpus.
186	- A full `docs/**` report is reviewed and labeled before enabling workflow
187	  hooks.
188	- Each rule has both positive and negative examples; no rule ships with only
189	  synthetic positives.
190	- Token-count improvements are reported separately from clarity findings so
191	  terse-but-ambiguous rewrites do not pass as quality improvements.
192	
193	### Phase 5: Agent Skill Upgrade
194	
195	Make agents use the prose workflow without reminders.
196	
197	Work:
198	
199	- Update `human-writing-support` to treat Vale-backed `ddx doc prose` as the
200	  normal post-edit check.
201	- Add examples for:
202	  - unsupported benefit claim -> concrete rewrite
203	  - AI-slop paragraph -> shorter actionable sentence
204	  - false positive in a table/path/API sample -> leave unchanged
205	  - token-cost edit -> remove filler while preserving meaning
206	- Instruct agents to rerun `ddx doc prose --changed` after edits.
207	- Instruct agents to report intentional exceptions only when findings remain.
208	
209	Acceptance:
210	
211	- Skill validation passes.
212	- A docs-edit dry run shows the agent runs the prose check, applies obvious
213	  edits, and leaves legitimate technical prose intact.
214	
215	Skill contents:
216	
217	- Trigger: any writing, rewriting, editing, or reviewing of Markdown under
218	  `docs/`.
219	- Required workflow:
220	  1. preserve the author's intent and DDx terminology;
221	  2. edit the document;
222	  3. run `ddx doc prose --changed`;
223	  4. apply high-signal findings;
224	  5. rerun `ddx doc prose --changed`;
225	  6. summarize remaining intentional exceptions.
226	- Preservation rules:
227	  - keep paths, commands, IDs, frontmatter, headings, table structure, API
228	    names, and acceptance criteria intact;
229	  - do not flatten useful technical density;

Perfect! Now I have a complete picture. Let me provide a comprehensive summary:

## Docprose Package Structure

The docprose package is a prose quality checker for DDx documentation. Here's the complete breakdown:

### Core Files

**1. `/cli/internal/docprose/assets.go` (125 lines)**
- Loads and parses YAML configuration files from the asset tree
- Key functions:
  - `loadDefaultConfig()` - Loads check.yaml
  - `loadRulePack(mode string)` - Loads mode-specific rules (technical, planning, public)
  - `loadDefaultVocabulary()` - Loads default vocabulary
- Defines type structures: `defaultConfig`, `ValeConfig`, `StylePack`, `StyleRule`, `rulePack`, `ruleSpec`, `Vocabulary`

**2. `/cli/internal/docprose/checker.go` (326 lines)**
- Main prose quality checker implementation
- Key types:
  - `Mode` - enum with values: `ModeTechnical`, `ModePlanning`, `ModePublic`
  - `Checker` - the main checker struct containing mode, rules, and vocabulary
- Key functions:
  - `NewChecker(mode Mode, vocabulary Vocabulary)` - Creates a new checker
  - `(c *Checker) Findings(file, text string) []Finding` - Analyzes text and returns findings
  - `(c *Checker) vocabularyFindings()` - Checks vocabulary reject terms
  - Helper functions: `matchesRule()`, `unsupportedClaim()`, `repeatedOpeningKey()`, `containsWord()`, etc.
- Detects: unsupported claims, repeated opening sentences, vocabulary violations, importance assertions

**3. `/cli/internal/docprose/finding.go` (13 lines)**
- Simple data structure for findings
- `Finding` struct with fields: `File`, `Line`, `RuleID`, `Severity`, `Rationale`, `SuggestedEdit`

**4. `/cli/internal/docprose/settings.go` (92 lines)**
- Settings management and layering
- Key functions:
  - `DefaultSettings()` - Returns packaged defaults
  - `ResolveSettings(cfg *ddxconfig.Config)` - Layers project config over defaults
- `Settings` struct aggregates all configuration

### Test Files

**5. `/cli/internal/docprose/docprose_test.go` (227 lines)**
- Unit tests covering:
  - `TestProseCheckerFindingSchema` - Verifies Finding schema completeness
  - `TestProseCheckerVocabularyOverrides` - Tests custom vocabulary
  - `TestProseCheckerVocabularyPreservation` - Verified excluded terms aren't flagged
  - `TestDefaultAssetLayout` - Validates asset files exist
  - `TestResolveSettingsLayersConfig` - Tests config layering
  - `TestProseCheckerChangedMode` - Tests all modes with fixtures
  - `TestProseCheckerPathMode` - Tests file path is preserved
  - `TestProseCheckerDoesNotFlagUsefulTechnicalContext` - Context-aware checking
  - `TestProseCheckerFlagsUnsupportedBenefitClaims` - Specific claim detection
- Contains helper functions: `sameFindings()`, `containsAll()`

**6. `/cli/internal/docprose/fixtures_test.go` (83 lines)**
- Fixture-based testing framework
- Key functions:
  - `loadFixtureCase(t, root, mode, name)` - Loads a single fixture
  - `listFixtureCases(t, root)` - Discovers all fixtures
  - `TestFixtures()` - Runs all fixtures with validation
- Validates: fixture structure, findings schema, all modes covered

### Testdata Directory Structure

Located at `/cli/internal/docprose/testdata/fixtures/`

**Existing Fixtures (5 test cases):**

1. **technical/generic-ai/**
   - `input.md` - Contains unsupported promotional language
   - `findings.golden.json` - 3 findings: claims, importance, repeated opening

2. **technical/technical-ddx/**
   - `input.md` - Well-written technical prose with project terms
   - `findings.golden.json` - Empty (no findings, serves as positive test)

3. **technical/vocabulary/**
   - `input.md` - Project terms (DDx, bead, Helix, Fizeau)
   - `findings.golden.json` - Empty (vocabulary preservation test)

4. **planning/generic-roadmap/**
   - `input.md` - Vague planning language
   - `findings.golden.json` - 3 findings: claims, repeated opening, vagueness

5. **public/generic-release/**
   - `input.md` - Marketing-style filler
   - `findings.golden.json` - 2 findings: claims, filler verbs

### Key Testing Patterns

The fixture testing pattern:
1. Each fixture lives in `testdata/fixtures/{mode}/{name}/`
2. Each fixture has:
   - `input.md` - Test input (must be multi-line markdown)
   - `findings.golden.json` - Expected findings array (sorted, JSON formatted)
3. Tests verify all modes are covered and schemas are complete

All fixtures are registered automatically via `listFixtureCases()`, which walks the testdata directory and sorts by mode then name.

# Product Vision: DDx

**Version:** 2.2.0
**Date:** 2026-05-01
**Status:** Active

## Lineage

DDx synthesizes three traditions, each grounded in prior art:

1. **Software factories** — model- and pattern-driven industrialization of software construction. DDx's framing of documents, templates, and tooling as the production line traces directly to Greenfield, Short, Cook, and Kent's 2004 *Software Factories* ([REF-007](references/REF-007-software-factories.md)).
2. **Spec-driven development** — executable specifications, not code, as the durable artifact AI agents consume. DDx inherits this stance from contemporary spec-first toolchains, notably GitHub Spec Kit ([REF-005](references/REF-005-github-spec-kit.md)) and AWS Kiro ([REF-006](references/REF-006-aws-kiro.md)).
3. **Continual improvement** — PDCA, ITIL continual improvement, and lean
   build-measure-learn loops all share the same control pattern: plan from
   current evidence, act, check the result, and adjust the operating model before the
   next cycle. DDx adapts that pattern to document-driven software work.
4. **Bounded-context agent execution** — quality decays as a single context
   window fills, well before the hard token limit. DDx's Ralph-loop /
   `ddx work` design responds to the empirical findings of *Lost in the
   Middle* ([REF-008](references/REF-008-lost-in-the-middle.md)) and Chroma's
   *Context Rot* ([REF-009](references/REF-009-context-rot.md)) by running each
   bead in a fresh, narrowly-scoped context against an explicit contract.

## Core Thesis

> Software is leverage. Fifty years of practice has shown that there are physics of software that must be respected to produce and maintain systems at scale. The agentic era allows every team to produce systems at scale and makes this even more important.
>
> Generative AI brings its own physics. DDx exists at the seam where these two meet — the fulcrum that lets software's lever do work. Without it, shipping software with agents quietly degrades into prompt-and-pray. The six principles below are the load-bearing claims on both sides.

## The Productivity Shift

Agentic AI compresses what used to be person-weeks of engineering into hours. The bottleneck moves: writing code is no longer the binding constraint — keeping intent, artifacts, and evidence coherent across many parallel agent invocations is. Teams that hit this new ceiling keep hitting the same six pain points, and every team independently rebuilds the same infrastructure to climb past them. DDx ships that infrastructure once.

Each pain point below maps to a specific DDx capability — the list is bounded by what the platform actually addresses, not an open-ended catalog.

1. **Context assembly is manual.** Composing the right persona + pattern + spec + prior evidence into agent context is ad hoc and slow. *(DDx: artifact library + composition.)*
2. **Documents drift silently.** When an upstream document changes, dependent artifacts go stale with no detection or reconciliation. *(DDx: artifact graph + staleness + reconciliation beads.)*
3. **Work tracking is reinvented.** Every workflow tool reimplements issue storage, dependency DAGs, and ready/waiting queues. *(DDx: bead tracker + JSONL interchange.)*
4. **Agent runs leave no shared trace.** Each tool grows its own dispatch, logging, and evidence shape; nothing carries between invocations. *(DDx: task execution lifecycle on a unified on-disk substrate.)*
5. **Cost of agentic work is invisible.** Token spend is a first-order constraint with no shared signal on the cheapest power band that reliably closes beads. *(DDx: power-aware retry evidence + token awareness.)*

# DDx Work: Operator Guide

This is the short operator reference for the `ddx run` / `ddx try` / `ddx work`
layers. For full flags, see `ddx run --help`, `ddx try --help`, and
`ddx work --help`.

## Which command do I run?

- **`ddx work`** — the queue-drain surface. It claims ready beads, invokes
  `ddx try`, records outcomes, and applies DDx-owned retry policy.
- **`ddx try <id>`** — one bead attempt in an isolated worktree. It wraps
  prompt construction, evidence capture, and merge/preserve finalization around
  one or more `ddx run` invocations.
- **`ddx run`** — one agent invocation atom. It calls upstream agent `Execute`
  exactly once with prompt/config, `MinPower`/`MaxPower`, and optional
  passthrough constraints.

DDx owns bead orchestration, evidence, success classification, and retry policy.
The upstream agent owns harness/provider/model routing and execution.

## Power and Passthrough

Use power bounds for normal dispatch:

```bash
ddx run --min-power 10 --prompt task.md
ddx work --top-power
```

`--harness`, `--provider`, and `--model` are passthrough constraints only. DDx
sends them unchanged to the agent and does not validate, fallback, rewrite, or
branch on them:

```bash
ddx run --min-power 10 --provider openrouter --model qwen3.6-27b --prompt task.md
```

If hard pins make the requested power bounds unsatisfiable, DDx records the
agent's typed error and stops with operator action required. It does not remove
pins or call route preflight to choose a substitute.

## Result Statuses

Every `ddx try` attempt reports a status in the attempt record. `ddx work`
uses these statuses plus DDx-owned evidence to decide close, preserve, stop, or
eligible retry.

| Status | Meaning | Work action |
|---|---|---|
| `success` | Agent produced changes and finalization succeeded | Close bead with evidence |
| `already_satisfied` | Acceptance was already met | Close bead with evidence |
| `no_changes` | Agent ran but produced no diff | Leave open; retry only if policy allows |
| `land_conflict` | Merge/finalization failed | Stop as operator-action or cooldown; do not power-retry |
| `post_run_check_failed` | Post-run checks failed after a valid attempt | Retry may raise `MinPower` if policy allows |
| `execution_failed` | Agent or environment errored | Classify before retry; deterministic setup failures do not power-retry |
| `structural_validation_failed` | Bead or prompt inputs invalid | Stop; fix tracker/spec input |

# real/ddx-docs - 30 cases (real excerpts from DDx documentation)
cat > "$BASE/real/ddx-docs/01-agentic-compression.md" << 'EOF'
Agentic AI compresses what used to be person-weeks of engineering into hours.
EOF

cat > "$BASE/real/ddx-docs/02-bottleneck-shift.md" << 'EOF'
The bottleneck moves: writing code is no longer the binding constraint — keeping intent, artifacts, and evidence coherent across many parallel agent invocations is.
EOF

cat > "$BASE/real/ddx-docs/03-ddx-ships-once.md" << 'EOF'
Every team independently rebuilds the same infrastructure to climb past them; DDx ships that infrastructure once.
EOF

cat > "$BASE/real/ddx-docs/04-software-leverage.md" << 'EOF'
Software is leverage.
EOF

cat > "$BASE/real/ddx-docs/05-context-assembly.md" << 'EOF'
Context assembly is manual; composing the right persona, pattern, spec, and prior evidence into agent context is ad hoc and slow.
EOF

cat > "$BASE/real/ddx-docs/06-documents-drift.md" << 'EOF'
Documents drift silently; when an upstream document changes, dependent artifacts go stale with no detection or reconciliation.
EOF

cat > "$BASE/real/ddx-docs/07-work-tracking-reinvented.md" << 'EOF'
Work tracking is reinvented by every workflow tool that reimplements issue storage, dependency DAGs, and ready or waiting queues.
EOF

cat > "$BASE/real/ddx-docs/08-agent-runs-no-trace.md" << 'EOF'
Agent runs leave no shared trace; each tool grows its own dispatch, logging, and evidence shape with nothing carrying between invocations.
EOF

cat > "$BASE/real/ddx-docs/09-cost-invisible.md" << 'EOF'
Cost of agentic work is invisible; token spend is a first-order constraint with no shared signal on the cheapest power band that reliably closes beads.
EOF

cat > "$BASE/real/ddx-docs/10-load-bearing-claims.md" << 'EOF'
The six principles below are the load-bearing claims on both sides of the DDx platform thesis.
EOF

cat > "$BASE/real/ddx-docs/11-ddx-owns-orchestration.md" << 'EOF'
DDx owns bead orchestration, evidence, success classification, and retry policy.
EOF

cat > "$BASE/real/ddx-docs/12-upstream-agent-owns.md" << 'EOF'
The upstream agent owns harness, provider, and model routing along with execution.
EOF

cat > "$BASE/real/ddx-docs/13-passthrough-unchanged.md" << 'EOF'
DDx sends passthrough constraints unchanged to the agent and does not validate, fallback, rewrite, or branch on them.
EOF

cat > "$BASE/real/ddx-docs/14-hard-pins-unsatisfiable.md" << 'EOF'
If hard pins make the requested power bounds unsatisfiable, DDx records the agent error and stops with operator action required.
EOF

cat > "$BASE/real/ddx-docs/15-three-project-stack.md" << 'EOF'
DDx is one layer in a three-project stack: DDx provides platform services; HELIX provides workflow methodology; Dun provides quality check execution.
EOF

cat > "$BASE/real/ddx-docs/16-monorepo-artifacts.md" << 'EOF'
This monorepo produces three artifacts: the ddx CLI binary, the ddx-server web endpoint, and the promotional site.
EOF

cat > "$BASE/real/ddx-docs/17-vale-subprocess.md" << 'EOF'
Vale runs per-file in a subprocess and returns structured output that the caller normalizes to the Finding schema.
EOF

cat > "$BASE/real/ddx-docs/18-embedded-checker.md" << 'EOF'
The embedded checker runs in-process and does not require Vale to be installed on the host system.
EOF

cat > "$BASE/real/ddx-docs/19-findings-normalized.md" << 'EOF'
Findings are normalized to a common schema before display or test assertion, regardless of which runner produced them.
EOF

cat > "$BASE/real/ddx-docs/20-cobra-command.md" << 'EOF'
Each CLI command is implemented as a separate Cobra command file, wired into the root command during package initialization.
EOF

cat > "$BASE/real/ddx-docs/21-beads-jsonl.md" << 'EOF'
Beads are stored in JSONL format at the project root and loaded into memory on first access within a command invocation.
EOF

cat > "$BASE/real/ddx-docs/22-persona-system.md" << 'EOF'
The persona system provides consistent AI personalities for different roles, such as code-reviewer and test-engineer.
EOF

cat > "$BASE/real/ddx-docs/23-worktree-cleanup.md" << 'EOF'
The worktree is cleaned up automatically after the execution completes or fails, unless the preserve flag is set.
EOF

cat > "$BASE/real/ddx-docs/24-retry-policy-owner.md" << 'EOF'
Retry policy is owned by the orchestrator, not the individual agent runner; runners report outcomes and the orchestrator decides next steps.
EOF

cat > "$BASE/real/ddx-docs/25-bead-archive.md" << 'EOF'
The bead archive stores migrated closed beads in append-only format and is created by the migrate subcommand.
EOF

cat > "$BASE/real/ddx-docs/26-drain-stops.md" << 'EOF'
Queue drain stops when no ready beads remain or a stop condition fires; the operator can re-trigger at any time.
EOF

cat > "$BASE/real/ddx-docs/27-rule-structure.md" << 'EOF'
Each rule specifies a severity level, a set of trigger phrases matched by substring, and a suggested edit shown in the finding output.
EOF

cat > "$BASE/real/ddx-docs/28-default-vocabulary.md" << 'EOF'
The default vocabulary accept list includes DDx-specific terms to prevent false positives on project names and artifact identifiers.
EOF

cat > "$BASE/real/ddx-docs/29-settings-layered.md" << 'EOF'
Settings are layered: package defaults are overridden by project configuration, which is overridden by command-line flags.
EOF

cat > "$BASE/real/ddx-docs/30-fixture-schema.md" << 'EOF'
Each test fixture has an input file and a golden findings file; the golden file is a JSON array of Finding objects sorted by line number.
EOF

echo "real/ddx-docs done"

Commit succeeded. All 4 ACs are verified:

1. **`TestDocProseCorpus_LoadsGroups`** — covers all 8 required corpus groups with minimum case counts (12/10/10/8 positive, 10/12/8 negative, 30 real).
2. **`TestDocProseCorpus_ExpectedFindingSchema`** — validates every `.expected.json` companion file: non-empty `required_rule_ids` array with all IDs starting with `"prose."`.
3. **`TestDocProseCorpus_NegativeStructureCasesStayQuiet`** — 30 negative cases across paths, commands, IDs, tables, code spans (`.jsonl`), frontmatter, and empirical-evidence prose all produce zero findings.
4. **`TestDocProseCorpus_PositiveCasesCanAssertRuleIDs`** — 22 gated positive cases (12 ai-slop + 10 unsupported-claim) each produce `prose.generic.claims`; aspirational token-cost and missing-actor-action cases are loaded but skipped without a gate.

`cd cli && go test ./internal/docprose/...` green, `lefthook run pre-commit` passes.
